g.text: · every term diting shows in the dashboard
The Wi-Fi name people choose from, such as Meituan or Guest. Many access points
The radio identity behind one SSID on one AP/radio. A single physical AP may
expose many BSSIDs when it broadcasts several SSIDs on 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz.
* Tx and Max use different Core
diting's best guess for the physical access point that owns a BSSID. Names you
set in ./aps.yaml (optional, next to aps.example.yaml in the repo) are most
accurate; ? labels are auto-inferred from MAC address patterns when no aps.yaml
Received signal strength. Less negative is stronger: -45 dBm is excellent, -65
dBm is usable, and around -75 dBm is weak.
Noise is background radio energy. SNR is signal minus noise; higher is better.
Low SNR can cause retries even when the AP is visible.
The radio range: 2.4 GHz reaches farther but is crowded; 5 GHz is faster with
shorter range; 6 GHz is newer, cleaner, and shorter range.
The slice of a band the AP is using. APs on the same or nearby channels share
airtime, so a quieter channel can help.
How much spectrum the AP uses, such as 20/40/80 MHz. Wider can be faster but
also easier to interfere with, especially on 2.4 GHz.
OPEN means no Wi-Fi-layer password/encryption. ENT means enterprise
authentication. WPA2/WPA3 are password or modern secured modes.
When the Mac moves from one BSSID to another. Same SSID does not guarantee the
↑/↓/PgUp/PgDn to scroll · Esc or b to close
